corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: The online setting is also interesting because it focuses on the optimization issues rather than on the small-sample regularization effects, so we decided to include in our experiments a synthetic images dataset inspired from and , from which as many examples as needed could be sampled, for testing the online learning scenario.
We call this dataset the Shapeset-3 × 2 dataset, with example images in Figure (top).

The MNIST digits , dataset has 50,000 training images, 10,000 validation images (for hyper-parameter selection), and 10,000 test images, each showing a 28×28 grey-scale pixel image of one of the 10 digits.

CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky & Hinton, 2009) is a labelled sub- set of the tiny-images dataset that contains 50,000 training examples (from which we extracted 10,000 as validation data) and 10,000 test examples.
Small-ImageNet which is a set of tiny 37×37 gray level images dataset computed from the higher-resolution and larger set at , with labels from the WordNet noun hierarchy.

Our objective here is to understand better why standard gradient descent from random initialization is doing so poorly with deep neural networks, to better understand these recent relative successes and help design better algorithms in the future.
Based on these considerations, we propose a new initialization scheme that brings substantially faster convergence.

Most of the recent experimental results with deep architecture are obtained with models that can be turned into deep supervised neural networks, but with initialization or training schemes different from the classical feedforward neural networks .
We noticed that for only one shape present in the image the task of recognizing it was too easy.

The task is to predict the objects present (e.g. triangle + ellipse, parallelogram + parallelogram, triangle alone, etc.) without having to distinguish between the foreground shape and the background shape when they overlap.
In parallel we need to extract the factors of variability that predict which object shapes are present.
Figure shows the evolution of the activation values (after the nonlinearity) at each hidden layer during training of a deep architecture with sigmoid activation functions.
We found that the plateaus in the training criterion (as a function of the parameters) are less present with the log-likelihood cost function.
